steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Sound travels through the air. What type of wave is a sound wave?
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Many students think sound is transverse because textbook diagrams show a wavy line. Sound is actually longitudinal — the air molecules vibrate back and forth parallel to the direction the wave travels, creating compressions and rarefactions.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Sound is a longitudinal wave. The air particles vibrate parallel to the direction of energy transfer, forming compressions (particles close together) and rarefactions (particles spread apart).
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 6.1.1 — Wave behaviour

steps.title: Transverse & Longitudinal Waves
steps.bodyHTML: All waves transfer energy without transferring matter. In a transverse wave, the oscillations are perpendicular (at right angles) to the direction of energy transfer — like shaking a slinky side to side, or ripples on water. In a longitudinal wave, the oscillations are parallel to the direction of energy transfer — like pushing a slinky back and forth. Longitudinal waves have compressions (particles squeezed together) and rarefactions (particles spread apart).
commonErrors: Saying sound is a transverse wave
Forgetting that waves transfer energy, not matter

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Drag the sliders to change frequency and amplitude. Watch how particles move in transverse and longitudinal waves.

aqa.questionText: Describe the difference between a transverse wave and a longitudinal wave. Give one example of each.

steps.label: Define transverse
steps.content: In a transverse wave, the oscillations are perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer.

steps.label: Define longitudinal
steps.content: In a longitudinal wave, the oscillations are parallel to the direction of energy transfer.

steps.label: Give examples
steps.content:
Transverse: light (or water waves, S-waves).
Longitudinal: sound (or ultrasound, P-waves).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — one correct example for each

aqa.finalAnswer: Transverse — perpendicular oscillations (e.g. light). Longitudinal — parallel oscillations (e.g. sound).

edexcel.questionText: Explain why sound cannot travel through a vacuum but light can.

steps.label: Sound needs a medium
steps.content: Sound is a longitudinal wave that requires particles to vibrate — it needs a medium (solid, liquid or gas).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — sound needs a medium / particles

steps.label: Light is electromagnetic
steps.content: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave that can travel through a vacuum — it does not need particles.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — EM waves do not need a medium

edexcel.finalAnswer: Sound needs particles to travel (mechanical wave); light is electromagnetic and does not need a medium.

ocr-gateway.questionText: State what is meant by a longitudinal wave.

steps.label: Define longitudinal wave
steps.content: A wave where the oscillations (vibrations) are parallel to the direction of energy transfer.

steps.label: Mention compressions and rarefactions
steps.content: It consists of a series of compressions and rarefactions.

ocr-gateway.finalAnswer: Oscillations parallel to direction of energy transfer, with compressions and rarefactions.

ocr-21c.questionText: Give two differences between transverse and longitudinal waves.

steps.label: First difference
steps.content: Transverse: oscillations perpendicular to direction of travel. Longitudinal: oscillations parallel.

steps.label: Second difference
steps.content: Longitudinal waves have compressions and rarefactions; transverse waves have crests and troughs.

ocr-21c.finalAnswer: Oscillation direction (perpendicular vs parallel) and features (crests/troughs vs compressions/rarefactions).

wjec.questionText: Name the type of wave where oscillations are at right angles to the direction of energy transfer. Give one example.

aqa.questionText: Which of the following is a longitudinal wave?
markSchemeSteps: Sound is longitudinal — particles vibrate parallel to direction of travel ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Water ripples look like they push forward, but they are transverse — the water moves up and down.

edexcel.questionText: Which of the following correctly describes a transverse wave?

options.label: A
options.text: Oscillations are parallel to the direction of energy transfer

options.label: B
options.text: Oscillations are perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer

options.label: C
options.text: The wave can only travel through solids

options.label: D
options.text: The wave has compressions and rarefactions

markSchemeSteps: Transverse — oscillations perpendicular to energy transfer ✓

ocr-gateway.questionText: A sound wave travels through air. In which direction do the air particles vibrate?

options.label: A
options.text: At right angles to the wave direction

options.label: B
options.text: In circles around the wave direction

markSchemeSteps: Sound is longitudinal — vibrations parallel to direction of travel ✓

ocr-21c.questionText: Which of the following is an example of a transverse wave?

wjec.questionText: What features are found in longitudinal waves but NOT in transverse waves?
markSchemeSteps: Compressions and rarefactions are unique to longitudinal waves ✓

equation.canonical: Transverse: oscillation ⟂ travel · Longitudinal: oscillation ∥ travel
equation.examTip: If the exam says 'mechanical wave', it means the wave needs a medium. Sound is mechanical. Light is NOT — it is electromagnetic.

boardNotes.aqa: Must know examples of each type. Common 1–2 mark question.
boardNotes.edexcel: Often tested with a diagram — label the oscillation direction.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: Know compressions and rarefactions for longitudinal waves.
boardNotes.ocr-21c: Link to seismic waves — P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse).
boardNotes.wjec: Describe using the words perpendicular and parallel for full marks.
